entities.description: A public structured text of thirty-one fresh manuscript-facsimile transcriptions, numbered and paginated to agree with Brook's 1948 edition but explicitly allowed to differ from Brook's readings.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item HarLyr
notes: U-M exposes thirty-one table-of-contents entries numbered 1-18 and 20-32.
The structured file has 31 DIV1, 31 HEAD, 1,563 L, 44 PB, and 45 EPB elements; printed page milestones run continuously from 29 through 72.
Brook 28 / Ker 69 alternates Middle English and Anglo-Norman lines even though the legacy root language declaration records only Middle English.
The XML header says printed front matter, back matter, and editorial notes were omitted.

entities.description: A composite parchment codex. Brook number 19 / Ker item 55 begins at f. 76r but is not part of the HarLyr selection.

reviewNotes: The primary subject is an edited digital selection of thirty-one heterogeneous anonymous lyrics, not one authored medieval work, all lyrics in Harley 2253, the complete Brook edition, the complete Ker facsimile, or the whole physical codex.

The selected lyric witnesses occupy discontiguous passages on Harley ff. 59v, 62v-63v, 66r-67r, 70v-73r, 75r-76r, 79r-83r, 106r, 114v-115r, and 128r-128v. Those folio surfaces also contain adjacent material, so the locators are not blanket content claims.

The U-M XML contains 1,563 L elements. The exact OTA deposit contains 1,562: the U-M form adds the line ‘ywraht wes of þe beste’ as line 34 in Ker item 46 and moves ‘Ientil iolyf so þe Iay’ from the OTA's duplicate line 34 to line 41, matching the later METS text. The precise revision history is undocumented.

Eleven U+FFFD replacement characters in the repository XML correspond to eleven legacy high bytes in the exact OTA payload. They are preserved as a documented encoding defect rather than silently reconstructed.

The source history says manuscript-joined words were segmented with a colon, for example ‘itelle’ represented as ‘i:telle.’ The editorial-practice vocabulary has no word-segmentation category, so this convention is documented here rather than mislabeled as punctuation normalization.

The legacy XML CREATION value ‘c. 1310’ is unsourced and is not adopted as either a common composition date or the witness date. The current British Library catalog dates the relevant manuscript part to circa 1330-1340; individual lyric composition dates remain unresolved.

Physical-codex coverage, printed-facsimile coverage, selected-work coverage, and digital-file coverage are recorded separately. In particular, the Royal folios in EETS 255 have no HarLyr target-text overlap.

The complete Royal 12 C XII IIIF bundle retains all 264 exposed surfaces from front cover through spine at 1800-pixel access resolution. Its work-portion mapping points only to Ker's ancillary ff. 68v and 76v and explicitly records zero target-work overlap.

Rights statements are resource-specific. U-M's current No Copyright-US display, OTA's CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license, the older embedded personal-scholarly-use notice, and the pinned CME repository's software-worded Unlicense are not collapsed into one blanket permission.
Negative access findings are dated 2026-07-14 and do not prove that licensed, private, onsite, or subsequently restored copies do not exist.

lineage.summary: The checked-in HarLyr XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item, which represents thirty-one newly made transcriptions of Harley lyrics, including one lyric that alternates Middle English and Anglo-Norman. The transcriptions were prepared from N. R. Ker's 1965 EETS monochrome facsimile under Frances McSparran's direction and corrected by Meredith Jones; they are not keyed from G. L. Brook's 1948 edition. The University of Virginia Electronic Text Center later imposed numbering and printed pagination conforming to Brook, which explains the sequence 1-18 and 20-32 and pages 29-72. Brook's number 19, the twenty-line multilingual ‘Dum ludis floribus’ at f. 76r, is omitted in full even though its last two lines are English. The 1965 facsimile reproduces only Harley ff. 49-140 and 142 plus Royal 12 C XII ff. 68v and 76v, but it covers every selected HarLyr witness passage. No lawful complete public online copy of that facsimile, and no current public Harley 2253 image set, was found. The British Library's complete 264-canvas Royal 12 C XII IIIF object is cached separately at practical access resolution because it includes Ker's two ancillary Royal leaves; neither leaf transmits HarLyr-selected text. Exact OTA transcript files and a later complete METS scholarly edition are also cached as distinct resources; none is evidence that HarLyr derives its readings from Brook.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous lyric authors
catalogSummary.date: Lyrics individually composed before their first known attestation in a Harley 2253 component copied circa 1330-1340; exact digital selection deposited in 1992 and electronically published by U-M in 1993
catalogSummary.editor: Frances McSparran; transcriptions corrected by Meredith Jones; University of Virginia Electronic Text Center numbering and pagination follow G. L. Brook
catalogSummary.region: Selected witnesses copied in or near Ludlow in the West Midlands; individual lyric composition places remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Harley Lyrics (selections)

catalogingSubject.label: Harley Lyrics (selections)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the exact thirty-one-item edited selection of fresh Harley manuscript-facsimile transcriptions deposited as OTA 1681 and represented by the U-M/General Corpus HarLyr encoding. Its texts are Middle English except that Brook 28 / Ker 69 alternates Middle English and Anglo-Norman. It comprises Brook lyric numbers 1-18 and 20-32, not one authored medieval work, not every English lyric in Harley 2253, not Brook number 19, not all texts in the relevant folios or manuscript, not Brook's complete 1948 edition, not Ker's complete 1965 printed facsimile, and not the U-M or repository XML considered merely as digital artifacts.

contentParts.label: Thirty-one selected Harley lyrics

locators.value: Facsimile plates corresponding to the same selected Harley folios

notes: The manuscript folio surfaces include adjacent and intervening texts not selected by HarLyr.
Brook number 19 / Ker item 55 at f. 76r is outside this part even though its final two lines are English.
Ker's two Royal 12 C XII comparison folios have no target-work overlap and are not content parts.
contentParts.summary: Thirty-one anonymous Middle English verse lyrics newly transcribed from Ker's 1965 facsimile and organized with Brook numbers 1-18 and 20-32. The items are heterogeneous in authorship, historical context, genre, and subject.

notes: The date belongs to the witness component and does not assign one composition date to thirty-one lyrics.

scope.description: First known attestation of the selected lyrics in the relevant Harley 2253 component

assertion.notes: Only a terminus from the witness is asserted; individual lyrics may be older and require separate research.

dateStatements.display: Individually unresolved; no later than first attestation circa 1330-1340

scope.description: The thirty-one historical lyric texts considered individually

notes: OTA records a deposit dated 3 April 1992; deposit is modeled separately as compilation evidence rather than automatically equated with public release.

scope.description: Exact fresh-transcription selection, not the first publication of the individual medieval lyrics

extent.description: The repository XML contains thirty-one top-level lyric divisions, thirty-one headings, and 1,563 L elements, with no LG or prose P elements in the body. Each division has continuous local line numbering. The numbered sequence follows Brook 1-18 and 20-32; the missing number 19 is an excluded complete multilingual poem, not an unencoded selected item. The exact OTA payload has 1,562 L-like elements because of one documented difference in Ker item 46. These are encoded-element counts, not a claim that every historical metrical line or stanza boundary is editorially settled.

formStatements.description: The body consists of 1,563 encoded verse-line elements and contains no prose body paragraphs.

scope.description: The whole cataloged unit consists of the thirty-one selected lyric texts

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Middle English is the primary language of the selection. The trilingual physical manuscript and the excluded multilingual Brook number 19 are not inherited wholesale; the separate Anglo-Norman statement is based on the included bilingual Ker item 69. Brief conventional Latin words such as ‘Amen’ are not modeled as another work language.

scope.description: All thirty-one selected lyrics

languageStatements.label: Anglo-Norman French
notes: The XML root declares only Middle English, but direct content inspection shows twenty-four alternating Anglo-Norman lines in Ker item 69.

scope.description: Alternating lines in Brook 28 / Ker 69, a bilingual Marian Passion lyric

notes: The cataloged unit follows the exact intellectual selection represented by OTA 1681 and HarLyr, not the broadest title string or physical manuscript boundary.
Brook supplies identifiers and locators, not the transcribed readings; describing HarLyr as simply encoded from Brook would contradict the explicit source history.
The relevant Harley folio ranges contain adjacent unselected texts and should not be interpreted as exhaustive surface coverage.
Royal 12 C XII is modeled only because two ancillary leaves occur in Ker's facsimile; it transmits no HarLyr-selected content.
Witness date and copying place are not composition date and place. Individual lyric research remains deliberately open.

The legacy XML CREATION value ‘c. 1310’ is unsourced and is not adopted as a common composition date or witness date; the current British Library circa 1330-1340 date is scoped to the relevant manuscript part.
Rights, access, and cached-copy integrity are recorded in the companion lineage manifest and must not be inherited across medieval text, modern edition, facsimile, image, and encoding layers.

next_steps: Research each Ker/BL item individually against current scholarship and witness catalogs.
Keep future assertions item-scoped rather than assigning one context to the edited selection.

next_steps: Ask EETS, the British Library, and holding libraries about licensed access or reproduction orders.
Recheck the official BL records and major digitization catalogs periodically.
openQuestions.question: Can a lawful complete digital copy of Ker's 1965 facsimile, or authorized images of all selected Harley folios, be obtained with clear reuse terms?

next_steps: Seek source-specific clarification from U-M, OTA, and the CME maintainer before commercial or unrestricted redistribution.
Keep medieval text, modern transcription, edition, image, and repository layers separate.

summaries.value: A thirty-one-item edited selection of anonymous Harley lyrics preserved in British Library MS Harley 2253. Middle English is primary, while Brook 28 / Ker 69 alternates Middle English and Anglo-Norman. Themes range across courtly love, seasonal nature, mortality, satire, penitence, Marian devotion, Christ's Passion, and salvation. The historical texts are heterogeneous and should not be assigned one author, composition date, or composition place.

The body has thirty-one verse divisions and 1,563 encoded lines. Division headings identify Brook lyrics 1-18 and 20-32 and Ker manuscript items 24b, 27-30, 33-36, 41, 43-46, 50-53, 60-67, 69, 73, 81, 92, and 93. Printed page milestones span Brook pp. 29-72.

Brook number 19 is deliberately absent. It is Ker item 55, ‘Dum ludis floribus,’ at Harley f. 76r: a twenty-line item with eighteen Latin/Anglo-Norman lines and two English closing lines. HarLyr excludes the whole multilingual item but retains Brook's subsequent numbering, so the gap defines an edited Middle-English-lyric selection rather than a lost XML division or an excerpted pair of English lines.

The exact OTA payload has 1,562 line elements; U-M has 1,563 because Ker item 46 gains one line and its following numbering is repaired. Eleven U-M replacement characters align with eleven legacy non-UTF-8 OTA bytes. The source history also says manuscript-joined words were segmented with a colon, for example ‘itelle’ as ‘i:telle.’ These are source-version and encoding-quality facts, not grounds for silent emendation. The U-M header says front matter, back matter, and printed editorial notes were omitted.

The lyric readings are new transcriptions made from Ker's 1965 EETS facsimile under McSparran's direction and corrected by Jones. Brook's 1948 edition supplies numbering, line references, and pagination only; HarLyr explicitly may differ from Brook. The 1965 volume is physically partial for Harley 2253 and Royal 12 C XII but complete for all HarLyr-selected witness passages. No lawful complete public online copy of that facsimile or current public Harley image set was found on 2026-07-14.

notes: The parenthetical boundary term comes from the OTA title and is retained because this is a thirty-one-item selection, not the complete set of Harley lyrics.
U-M and repository display title; it can suggest a broader boundary than the body actually encodes.

work.author: Anonymous lyric authors
work.dateDisplay: Lyrics individually composed before their first known attestation in a Harley 2253 component copied circa 1330-1340; exact digital selection deposited in 1992 and electronically published by U-M in 1993
work.editor: Frances McSparran; transcriptions corrected by Meredith Jones; University of Virginia Electronic Text Center numbering and pagination follow G. L. Brook
work.regionDisplay: Selected witnesses copied in or near Ludlow in the West Midlands; individual lyric composition places remain unresolved
work.summary: A thirty-one-item edited selection of anonymous Harley lyrics preserved in British Library MS Harley 2253. Middle English is primary, while Brook 28 / Ker 69 alternates Middle English and Anglo-Norman. Themes range across courtly love, seasonal nature, mortality, satire, penitence, Marian devotion, Christ's Passion, and salvation. The historical texts are heterogeneous and should not be assigned one author, composition date, or composition place.
